{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

[[Category:qq]][[en:Shikinen-sai]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikinen-sai}}

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:Completion tier 1]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

[[Category:11]][[en:Shikinen-sai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Shikinen-sai]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Shikinen-sai}}[[Category:11]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikinen-sai}}

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

[[Category:qq]][[sn:Shikinen-sai]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shikinen-sai]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shikinen-sai}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior
{{Shinto-stub}}

Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. {{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|12=draft}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|12=draft}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭|12=draft}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|12=draft}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭|12=draft}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|12=draft}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神|12=draft}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|12=draft}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀|12=draft}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮|12=draft}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|12=draft}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|12=draft}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭|12=draft}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|12=draft}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮|12=draft}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|12=draft}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社|12=draft}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|12=draft}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

18 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:式年祭 up to rev 88849656
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:49 (UTC)}} '''式年祭'''（しきねんさい）は、決められた期間ごとに行われる[[祭祀]]のことであり、[[神社]]の祭礼や祖先祭祀において見られる祭祀形態である。
由緒ある神社の一部では、定まった年ごとに行われる祭祀がある。例えば、[[鹿島神宮]]、[[香取神宮]]では12年ごとの[[午]]の年に、盛大な[[神幸祭]]を行うこととなっている。また、[[諏訪大社]]でも7年ごと（開催年を1年と数えるため実際の周期は6年）の[[寅]]もしくは[[申]]の年に[[御柱祭]]が行われることは有名である。これらの祭りは毎年行われる祭り（[[例祭]]）よりも大規模であることが多く、重視されている祭りといえる。
神社には、一定の年ごとに社殿の建て直し（式年[[遷宮]]）をするところがみられるが、社殿の建て直し自体が祭祀の一環だという見方をしたとき、これも式年祭の一種と言える。最も有名なのは伊勢[[神宮式年遷宮]]で、20年ごとに全ての社殿を建て直し、大規模な祭礼を行うことになっている。あまり注目されないが、諏訪大社の御柱祭もその儀礼の中には社殿の建て直しが含まれており、御柱祭も式年遷宮に含めることができる。なお、式年祭は大社に限らず、[[氏神]]レベルでの神社においても、その神社独自の由緒に基づいて、式年祭を行っているところがある。
一方、祖先祭祀においても、決められた年ごとに祭祀を行うことが多い。一般に[[仏教]]行事として行われる[[年忌]]では、一周忌・三回忌・七回忌・十三回忌・十七回忌・二十三回忌などと特定の年（等間隔ではない）に、通常の祭祀とは違った特別な祭祀が行われる。
[[宮中祭祀]]における式年は、3年・5年・10年・20年・30年・40年・50年・以後100年ごとと規定され、祖先祭祀に関するものにおいて式年の大祭と小祭が神式で行われている。
人物を祀る[[日光東照宮]]や[[北野天満宮]]では50年ごとに大祭が行われており、これは両者の結合ともいうことができるかもしれない。
==主な式年祭== *[[鹿島神宮]]：御船祭 *[[香取神宮]]：[[神幸祭]] *[[諏訪大社]]：[[御柱祭]] *[[伊勢神宮]]：[[神宮式年遷宮]] *[[北野天満宮]]：大萬燈祭 *[[日光東照宮]]：式年祭 *[[戸隠神社]]：式年大祭 *[[香椎宮]]：臨時奉幣祭 *[[宇佐神宮]]：臨時奉幣祭 ===宮中祭祀の式年祭=== ;大祭 *先帝以前3代の式年祭 *先后の式年祭 *皇妣たる皇后の式年祭 ;小祭 *[[綏靖天皇]]以下先帝以前4代に至る歴代天皇の式年祭
{{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しきねんさい}} [[Category:日本の祭り]] [[Category:神道行事|*]] [[Category:民俗学]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[式年祭]] to [[Shikinen-sai]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:49 (UTC)}} '''式年祭'''（しきねんさい）は、決められた期間ごとに行われる[[祭祀]]のことであり、[[神社]]の祭礼や祖先祭祀において見られる祭祀形態である。
由緒ある神社の一部では、定まった年ごとに行われる祭祀がある。例えば、[[鹿島神宮]]、[[香取神宮]]では12年ごとの[[午]]の年に、盛大な[[神幸祭]]を行うこととなっている。また、[[諏訪大社]]でも7年ごと（開催年を1年と数えるため実際の周期は6年）の[[寅]]もしくは[[申]]の年に[[御柱祭]]が行われることは有名である。これらの祭りは毎年行われる祭り（[[例祭]]）よりも大規模であることが多く、重視されている祭りといえる。
神社には、一定の年ごとに社殿の建て直し（式年[[遷宮]]）をするところがみられるが、社殿の建て直し自体が祭祀の一環だという見方をしたとき、これも式年祭の一種と言える。最も有名なのは伊勢[[神宮式年遷宮]]で、20年ごとに全ての社殿を建て直し、大規模な祭礼を行うことになっている。あまり注目されないが、諏訪大社の御柱祭もその儀礼の中には社殿の建て直しが含まれており、御柱祭も式年遷宮に含めることができる。なお、式年祭は大社に限らず、[[氏神]]レベルでの神社においても、その神社独自の由緒に基づいて、式年祭を行っているところがある。
一方、祖先祭祀においても、決められた年ごとに祭祀を行うことが多い。一般に[[仏教]]行事として行われる[[年忌]]では、一周忌・三回忌・七回忌・十三回忌・十七回忌・二十三回忌などと特定の年（等間隔ではない）に、通常の祭祀とは違った特別な祭祀が行われる。
[[宮中祭祀]]における式年は、3年・5年・10年・20年・30年・40年・50年・以後100年ごとと規定され、祖先祭祀に関するものにおいて式年の大祭と小祭が神式で行われている。
人物を祀る[[日光東照宮]]や[[北野天満宮]]では50年ごとに大祭が行われており、これは両者の結合ともいうことができるかもしれない。
==主な式年祭== *[[鹿島神宮]]：御船祭 *[[香取神宮]]：[[神幸祭]] *[[諏訪大社]]：[[御柱祭]] *[[伊勢神宮]]：[[神宮式年遷宮]] *[[北野天満宮]]：大萬燈祭 *[[日光東照宮]]：式年祭 *[[戸隠神社]]：式年大祭 *[[香椎宮]]：臨時奉幣祭 *[[宇佐神宮]]：臨時奉幣祭 ===宮中祭祀の式年祭=== ;大祭 *先帝以前3代の式年祭 *先后の式年祭 *皇妣たる皇后の式年祭 ;小祭 *[[綏靖天皇]]以下先帝以前4代に至る歴代天皇の式年祭
{{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しきねんさい}} [[Category:日本の祭り]] [[Category:神道行事|*]] [[Category:民俗学]]

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀|1=Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|lt=Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace|qq=draft|qq=draft}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Shikinen-sai}}
{{translated page|ja|式年祭|version=88849656|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Shikinen-sai]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Japanese festivals]] [[Category:Shinto rituals|*]] [[Category:Folklore]] [[Category:式年祭]] }}
[[sn:Shikinen-sai]] [[ja:式年祭]] [[en:Shikinen-sai]]

== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
