revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori Shrine|qq=}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkō-sai|qq=}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa-taisha|qq=}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onbashira Festival|qq=}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujigami|qq=}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|qq=}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀|1=Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|lt=Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|qq=}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano Tenman-gū|qq=}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Shrine|qq=}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori Shrine|qq=}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkō-sai|qq=}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa-taisha|qq=}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onbashira Festival|qq=}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano Tenman-gū|qq=}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togakushi Shrine|qq=}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashii Shrine|qq=}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Shrine|qq=}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior
{{Shinto-stub}}

These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Jingu|lt=Kashima Jingu|lt=Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Katori Jingu|lt=Katori Jingu|lt=Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori Shrine|qq=}}, grand {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkō-sai|qq=}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa-taisha|qq=}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onbashira Festival|qq=}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|ja|氏神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujigami|qq=}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|qq=}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Imperial Household|ja|宮中祭祀|1=Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|lt=Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|qq=}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|lt=Kitano Tenmangū|lt=Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano Tenman-gū|qq=}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Jingu|lt=Kashima Jingu|lt=Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Shrine|qq=}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Jingu|lt=Katori Jingu|lt=Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori Shrine|qq=}}: {{ill|Shinkō-sai|ja|神幸祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkō-sai|qq=}} * {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa-taisha|qq=}}: {{ill|Onbashira Festival|ja|御柱祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onbashira Festival|qq=}} * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|lt=Kitano Tenmangū|lt=Kitano Tenman-gū|ja|北野天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano Tenman-gū|qq=}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|ja|戸隠神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togakushi Shrine|qq=}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashii Shrine|qq=}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Shrine|ja|宇佐神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Shrine|qq=}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior
{{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] and [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]], grand [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]], the well-known [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ([[:en:Ujigami|Ujigami]]) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|qq=}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] and [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]], hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:Festivals in Japan
revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] and [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]], grand [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]], the well-known [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ([[:en:Ujigami|Ujigami]]) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|qq=}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] and [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]], hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]]: Funamatsuri * [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]]: [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]]: [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]: Grand Manto Festival * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]: Shikinen-sai * [[:en:Togakushi Shrine|Togakushi Shrine]]: Grand Shikinen Festival * [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior
{{Shinto-stub}}

== Major Shikinen-sai == * [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]]: Funamatsuri * [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]]: [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]]: [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]: Grand Manto Festival * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]: Shikinen-sai * [[:en:Togakushi Shrine|Togakushi Shrine]]: Grand Shikinen Festival * [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] and [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]], grand [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]], the well-known [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ([[:en:Ujigami|Ujigami]]) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[Buddhist]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|qq=}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] and [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]], hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]]: Funamatsuri * [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]]: [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]]: [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]: Grand Manto Festival * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]: Shikinen-sai * [[:en:Togakushi Shrine|Togakushi Shrine]]: Grand Shikinen Festival * [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] and [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]], hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]]: Funamatsuri * [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]]: [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]]: [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]: Grand Manto Festival * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]: Shikinen-sai * [[:en:Togakushi Shrine|Togakushi Shrine]]: Grand Shikinen Festival * [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[Shinto shrines]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] and [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]], grand [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[:en:Year of the Horse|Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]], the well-known [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[Year of the Tiger]] or [[Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ([[:en:Ujigami|Ujigami]]) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|qq=}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] and [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]], hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.

=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

Commonly observed in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|qq=}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] and [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]], hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]]: Funamatsuri * [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]]: [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]]: [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]: Grand Manto Festival * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]: Shikinen-sai * [[:en:Togakushi Shrine|Togakushi Shrine]]: Grand Shikinen Festival * [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] and [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]], grand [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[:en:Year of the Horse|Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]], the well-known [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[:en:Year of the Tiger|Year of the Tiger]] or [[:en:Year of the Monkey|Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ([[:en:Ujigami|Ujigami]]) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|qq=}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] and [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]], hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]]: Funamatsuri * [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]]: [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]]: [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]: Grand Manto Festival * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]: Shikinen-sai * [[:en:Togakushi Shrine|Togakushi Shrine]]: Grand Shikinen Festival * [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:式年祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487032]] Wikidata name: shikinen-sai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] and [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]], grand [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[:en:Year of the Horse|Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]], the well-known [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[:en:Year of the Tiger|Year of the Tiger]] or [[:en:Year of the Monkey|Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ([[:en:Ujigami|Ujigami]]) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|qq=|1=Nenki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483842}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] and [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]], hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]]: Funamatsuri * [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]]: [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]]: [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]: Grand Manto Festival * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]: Shikinen-sai * [[:en:Togakushi Shrine|Togakushi Shrine]]: Grand Shikinen Festival * [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior
{{Shinto-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:式年祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487032]] Wikidata name: shikinen-sai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] and [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]], grand [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[:en:Year of the Horse|Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]], the well-known [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[:en:Year of the Tiger|Year of the Tiger]] or [[:en:Year of the Monkey|Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ([[:en:Ujigami|Ujigami]]) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|1=Nenki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483842}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] and [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]], hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]]: Funamatsuri * [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]]: [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]]: [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]: Grand Manto Festival * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]: Shikinen-sai * [[:en:Togakushi Shrine|Togakushi Shrine]]: Grand Shikinen Festival * [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] and [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]], grand [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[:en:Year of the Horse|Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]], the well-known [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[:en:Year of the Tiger|Year of the Tiger]] or [[:en:Year of the Monkey|Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ([[:en:Ujigami|Ujigami]]) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|1=Nenki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483842}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] and [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]], hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]]: Funamatsuri * [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]]: [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]]: [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]: Grand Manto Festival * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]: Shikinen-sai * [[:en:Togakushi Shrine|Togakushi Shrine]]: Grand Shikinen Festival * [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|式年祭|version=88849656|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Commonly observed in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|1=Nenki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483842}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] and [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]], hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]]: Funamatsuri * [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]]: [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]]: [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]: Grand Manto Festival * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]: Shikinen-sai * [[:en:Togakushi Shrine|Togakushi Shrine]]: Grand Shikinen Festival * [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:式年祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:式年祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487032]] Wikidata name: shikinen-sai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] and [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]], grand [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[:en:Year of the Horse|Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]], the well-known [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[:en:Year of the Tiger|Year of the Tiger]] or [[:en:Year of the Monkey|Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ([[:en:Ujigami|Ujigami]]) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|1=Nenki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483842}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] and [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]], hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]]: Funamatsuri * [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]]: [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]]: [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]: Grand Manto Festival * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]: Shikinen-sai * [[:en:Togakushi Shrine|Togakushi Shrine]]: Grand Shikinen Festival * [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11487032}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Folklore]] [[Category:Folklore studies]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:式年祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:式年祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:式年祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487032]] Wikidata name: shikinen-sai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] and [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]], grand [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] ceremonies are held every 12 years during the [[:en:Year of the Horse|Year of the Horse]]. Similarly, at [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]], the well-known [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[:en:Year of the Tiger|Year of the Tiger]] or [[:en:Year of the Monkey|Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ([[:en:Ujigami|Ujigami]]) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|1=Nenki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483842}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] and [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]], hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]]: Funamatsuri * [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]]: [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkō-sai]] * [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]]: [[:en:Onbashira|Onbashira Festival]] * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * [[:en:Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]: Grand Manto Festival * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]: Shikinen-sai * [[:en:Togakushi Shrine|Togakushi Shrine]]: Grand Shikinen Festival * [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival * [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Shrine]]: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|式年祭|version=88849656|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories|
}}

<!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--><!--Shikinen-sai--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11487032}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Folklore]] [[Category:Folklore studies]] [[Category:Shinto festivals]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:式年祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:式年祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:式年祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487032]] Wikidata name: shikinen-sai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|en|Kashima Shrine|WD=Q706499|lt=Kashima Shrine}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|en|Katori Shrine|WD=Q372380|lt=Katori Shrine}}, grand {{ill|Shinko-shiki|en|Shinko-shiki|WD=Q123907414|lt=Shinkō-sai}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the {{ill|Year of the Horse|en|Year of the Horse|WD=Q1753426|lt=Year of the Horse}}. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira|en|Onbashira|WD=Q3698846|lt=Onbashira Festival}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[:en:Year of the Tiger|Year of the Tiger]] or [[:en:Year of the Monkey|Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|en|Ujigami|WD=Q767655|lt=Ujigami}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|1=Nenki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483842}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|en|Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|WD=Q11453226|lt=Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|en|Kitano Tenmangū|WD=Q662176|lt=Kitano Tenman-gū}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|en|Kashima Shrine|WD=Q706499|lt=Kashima Shrine}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|en|Katori Shrine|WD=Q372380|lt=Katori Shrine}}: {{ill|Shinko-shiki|en|Shinko-shiki|WD=Q123907414|lt=Shinkō-sai}} * {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira|en|Onbashira|WD=Q3698846|lt=Onbashira Festival}} * {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|en|Kitano Tenmangū|WD=Q662176|lt=Kitano Tenman-gū}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|en|Togakushi Shrine|WD=Q218828|lt=Togakushi Shrine}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii-gū|en|Kashii-gū|WD=Q631373|lt=Kashii Shrine}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Shrine}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|式年祭|version=88849656|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:式年祭]]

== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|en|Kashima Shrine|WD=Q706499|lt=Kashima Shrine|1=Kashima Jingū}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|en|Katori Shrine|WD=Q372380|lt=Katori Shrine|1=Katori Jingū}}: {{ill|Shinko-shiki|en|Shinko-shiki|WD=Q123907414|lt=Shinkō-sai|1=Shinko-shiki}} * {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira|en|Onbashira|WD=Q3698846|lt=Onbashira Festival|1=Onbashira}} * {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|en|Kitano Tenmangū|WD=Q662176|lt=Kitano Tenman-gū|1=Kitano Tenmangū}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|en|Togakushi Shrine|WD=Q218828|lt=Togakushi Shrine|1=Togakushi Shrine}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii-gū|en|Kashii-gū|WD=Q631373|lt=Kashii Shrine|1=Kashii-gū}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Shrine|1=Usa Jingū}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival

{{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}} '''Shikinen-sai''' (式年祭) refers to religious rituals performed at fixed intervals, often seen in the festivals of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} and ancestral worship ceremonies.
Some shrines with significant historical lineage conduct rituals at predetermined intervals. For instance, at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|en|Kashima Shrine|WD=Q706499|lt=Kashima Shrine|1=Kashima Jingū}} and {{ill|Katori Shrine|en|Katori Shrine|WD=Q372380|lt=Katori Shrine|1=Katori Jingū}}, grand {{ill|Shinko-shiki|en|Shinko-shiki|WD=Q123907414|lt=Shinkō-sai|1=Shinko-shiki}} ceremonies are held every 12 years during the {{ill|Year of the Horse|en|Year of the Horse|WD=Q1753426|lt=Year of the Horse|1=Year of the Horse (film)}}. Similarly, at {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}}, the well-known {{ill|Onbashira|en|Onbashira|WD=Q3698846|lt=Onbashira Festival|1=Onbashira}} takes place every seven years (counting the year of the festival as the first year, making the actual interval six years) during the [[:en:Year of the Tiger|Year of the Tiger]] or [[:en:Year of the Monkey|Year of the Monkey]]. These festivals are often larger and more emphasized than the annual {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} and hold a higher degree of significance.
In some cases, shrines reconstruct their buildings at fixed intervals as part of their rituals. This reconstruction is considered an integral part of the religious ceremonies and can be categorized as a type of Shikinen-sai. The most famous example is the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}'s {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}, where all the shrine buildings are reconstructed every 20 years in a grand ceremony. Another example is the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha, which includes elements of shrine rebuilding and can also be regarded as a form of Shikinen Sengū. Additionally, Shikinen-sai are not exclusive to grand shrines; local community shrines ({{ill|Ujigami|en|Ujigami|WD=Q767655|lt=Ujigami|1=Ujigami}}) may also conduct these rituals based on their unique traditions and historical significance.
In ancestral worship, ceremonies are also performed at specific intervals. Commonly observed in {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} practices, these ceremonies, known as {{ill|Nenki|ja|年忌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenki|1=Buddhist anniversary service|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11483842}}, occur on particular years such as the first anniversary, third, seventh, thirteenth, seventeenth, and twenty-third anniversaries, among others. These rituals differ from routine ceremonies and hold special significance.
In {{ill|Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|en|Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|WD=Q11453226|lt=Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace|1=Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} rites, Shikinen-sai are regulated for intervals of 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 years, and subsequently every 100 years. These rituals, related to ancestral worship, include major and minor ceremonies performed in the Shinto style.
Shrines dedicated to historical figures, such as {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} and {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|en|Kitano Tenmangū|WD=Q662176|lt=Kitano Tenman-gū|1=Kitano Tenmangū}}, hold grand festivals every 50 years. These ceremonies could be seen as a fusion of ancestral worship and Shinto rituals.
== Major Shikinen-sai == * {{ill|Kashima Shrine|en|Kashima Shrine|WD=Q706499|lt=Kashima Shrine|1=Kashima Jingū}}: Funamatsuri * {{ill|Katori Shrine|en|Katori Shrine|WD=Q372380|lt=Katori Shrine|1=Katori Jingū}}: {{ill|Shinko-shiki|en|Shinko-shiki|WD=Q123907414|lt=Shinkō-sai|1=Shinko-shiki}} * {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}}: {{ill|Onbashira|en|Onbashira|WD=Q3698846|lt=Onbashira Festival|1=Onbashira}} * {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}: {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} * {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|en|Kitano Tenmangū|WD=Q662176|lt=Kitano Tenman-gū|1=Kitano Tenmangū}}: Grand Manto Festival * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}: Shikinen-sai * {{ill|Togakushi Shrine|en|Togakushi Shrine|WD=Q218828|lt=Togakushi Shrine|1=Togakushi Shrine}}: Grand Shikinen Festival * {{ill|Kashii-gū|en|Kashii-gū|WD=Q631373|lt=Kashii Shrine|1=Kashii-gū}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival * {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Shrine|1=Usa Jingū}}: Temporary Hōhei Festival
=== Shikinen-sai in Imperial Household Rites === ; Grand Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for the last three generations of emperors * Shikinen-sai for previous empresses * Shikinen-sai for empresses who are the imperial mothers ; Minor Ceremonies * Shikinen-sai for emperors from Emperor Suizei to the fourth generation prior
